title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review: V2B - A Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Single Object Tracking in Sparse Point Clouds  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces V2B, a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. Its core contributions are twofold: (1) a shape-aware feature learning network leveraging dense shape generation to enhance target discrimination, and (2) a voxel-to-BEV localization network that directly regresses target centers from compressed bird's-eye-view features without proposals. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in sparse scenarios, with significant gains over methods like SC3D and P2B.  

### Main Review  
**Strengths and Technical Soundness:**  
The paper addresses the critical challenge of object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds—a problem of significant importance for applications like autonomous driving. The submission is clearly written, well-organized, and provides sufficient technical detail. The methodology is technically sound, featuring a novel integration of shape-aware feature learning (via template feature embedding and dense point cloud generation) and anchor-free BEV-based center regression. This combination effectively differentiates V2B from proposal-based predecessors. The motivation for key designs is well-articulated, and the experimental results are largely impressive, showing substantial improvements (e.g., +8–10% Success on KITTI) over prior arts. Ablation studies convincingly validate components like voxel size selection and template schemes.  

**Concerns and Unsupported Claims:**  
However, several claims lack full experimental justification. Most notably, the assertion that V2B excels in sparse point clouds is not fully supported. As observed in Table 1 vs. Table 2, when evaluating sparse objects, *only* the proposed method suffers a performance drop, while baselines like SC3D and P2B improve. This contradicts the claim that the shape-aware feature enhances robustness to sparsity. Additionally, critical ablation studies are incomplete:  
1. The necessity of using *both* global and local features in the template feature embedding and shape-aware modules lacks verification.  
2. Ablations are limited to the *car* category (KITTI), neglecting other categories (nuScenes), reducing generalizability.  
3. Hyperparameters like loss weights (λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) and the gate mechanism (Eq. 5) lack sensitivity analysis.  

While the overall results are strong, the paper would benefit from deeper failure-case analysis and comparisons to newer works (e.g., post-2021 methods). Some phrasing (e.g., "stable does not mean accurate," Line 378–380) requires clarification.  

**Clarity and Reproducibility:**  
The paper is well-structured, with clear figures illustrating architectures. However, Section 3.1.2 should clarify *why shape encoding is discarded during inference*, and Figures 3–4 could better visualize feature flow. Reproducibility is facilitated via code release and training details, though computational costs (training/inference latency) remain unquantified.  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
Key limitations include:  
1. **Sparsity Handling:** The method’s efficacy in extreme sparsity requires further validation.  
2. **Scope:** BEV representation may be insufficient for indoor scenes or complex object proposals.  
3. **Efficiency:** Computational costs of shape completion/voxelization are noted, but inference latency versus baselines is unreported—critical for real-time systems.  
4. **Generalization:** Sensitivity to voxel size hyperparameters and unseen object categories needs testing.  

Societal impacts are unaddressed. Authors should discuss:  
- Ethical implications of tracking failures in safety-critical applications (e.g., autonomous vehicles).  
- Potential misuse risks (e.g., surveillance).  
- Energy consumption of LiDAR systems.  

**Recommendation:**  
This work presents a compelling advancement in 3D SOT, with a novel architecture delivering SOTA results. While the core contributions are significant, the identified gaps in experimental validation and analysis temper enthusiasm. **I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7 - Good paper)** pending author revisions addressing:  
1. Experimental justification for sparse-scenario claims.  
2. Comprehensive ablations across all categories and key hyperparameters.  
3. Clarification of limitations (BEV suitability, computational trade-offs).  
4. Addition of ethical considerations.  

With these revisions, the paper’s impact will be fully realized.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary  
This paper proposes V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking that localizes targets using a template and search space. The method integrates a *shape-aware feature learning network* to enhance target discrimination through dense point cloud generation and a *voxel-to-BEV target localization network* that regresses target centers directly from compressed bird’s-eye view (BEV) features. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in sparse scenarios, with significant gains over predecessors like P2B and SC3D.  

---

### Overall Assessment  
The proposed method achieves compelling performance improvements in both accuracy and computational efficiency over existing works. However, concerns regarding writing quality, novelty validation, methodological depth, and broader impact analysis necessitate major revisions before acceptance.  

#### Strengths  
1. **Performance Gains**: The method achieves a noticeable margin over state-of-the-art trackers (e.g., +8.9–10% Success on KITTI) while maintaining computational efficiency, supported by thorough benchmarking.  
2. **Innovative Training Mechanism**: The shape-aware feature learning network effectively leverages dense point cloud generation *during training* to enhance feature expressiveness in sparse scenarios—an intriguing contribution.  
3. **Efficient Inference**: The voxel-to-BEV approach eliminates proposal generation, enabling direct center regression in BEV space, which streamlines inference.  
4. **Rigorous Evaluation**: Experiments across datasets (KITTI/nuScenes) and sparse/dense splits robustly validate claims. The inclusion of an ablation study for voxel size and template schemes strengthens technical soundness.  

#### Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. **Writing Quality**: The manuscript contains pervasive grammatical errors and unpolished sentences (e.g., unclear syntax in Sections 3.1–3.2). Thorough proofreading is essential.  
2. **Novelty Justification**:  
   - The **Template Feature Embedding Network** is a complex module with "heavy engineering." While ablation studies exist for voxelization and template schemes, critical components (e.g., global/local feature interplay, distance functions for similarity mapping) lack validation. Given the conference’s standards, deeper theoretical analysis is needed to justify its complexity.  
   - The **Voxel-to-BEV Localization** module closely mirrors established 3D detection encoders (e.g., PointPillar, SECOND). The paper inadequately distinguishes its adaptation for tracking, requiring expanded citations and architectural differentiation.  
3. **Under-Explored Methodological Elements**:  
   - **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: Loss weights (λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) and voxel size selections significantly impact performance but lack ablation analysis.  
   - **Inference Efficiency**: While computational cost analysis is provided, inference latency comparisons against baselines are absent—a notable gap given the focus on real-world applicability.  
4. **Incomplete Analysis of Limitations**:  
   - Failure modes (e.g., re-identification, tracklet initialization) are briefly mentioned but not rigorously studied.  
   - Generalization to unseen object categories and robustness to extreme sparsity require further validation.  
5. **Neglected Societal Impact**: Potential risks (e.g., tracking failures in autonomous driving, surveillance misuse) and ethical guidelines for deployment are unaddressed—a critical omission for applied research.  

---

### Limitations and Societal Impact  
The paper acknowledges sparsity limitations in supplementary material but does not thoroughly examine:  
- **Operational Constraints**: Computational costs of shape completion during training and sensitivity to voxelization hyperparameters.  
- **Safety-Critical Risks**: No discussion of real-world consequences (e.g., autonomous vehicle collisions due to tracking drift).  
- **Ethical Considerations**: Broader implications like privacy violations from enhanced tracking or energy consumption of LiDAR systems are overlooked. Authors must expand this section to address ethical deployment and failure mitigation.  

---

### Recommendations for Revision  
1. **Writing**: Engage a language editing service to resolve grammatical issues.  
2. **Novelty**:  
   - Clarify distinctions between the Voxel-to-BEV module and prior 3D detection encoders (e.g., PointPillar).  
   - Strengthen ablation studies for the feature embedding network (e.g., component-level efficacy, distance function choices).  
3. **Methodology**:  
   - Analyze hyperparameter sensitivity (loss weights, voxel sizes).  
   - Benchmark inference latency against baselines.  
4. **Evaluation**: Include failure case analysis (e.g., qualitative visualizations of tracking drift).  
5. **Broader Impact**: Add a dedicated section discussing ethical guidelines, safety risks, and societal implications.  

---

### Rating  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper’s empirical strengths and efficiency gains position it above the acceptance threshold. However, unresolved issues in novelty substantiation, technical depth, and impact analysis preclude a higher rating. *Conditional acceptance is recommended pending major revisions*.

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

**Summary**  
This paper introduces V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker for 3D object tracking in autonomous driving, designed to handle sparse LiDAR point clouds. The method combines a shape-aware feature learning network (enhancing target discrimination via dense shape generation) with a voxel-to-BEV localization network that regresses target centers directly from compressed bird's-eye view features. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in sparse scenarios, with significant gains over prior methods like P2B and SC3D.  

### **Strengths**  
- **Novelty & Technical Soundness**: The integration of shape completion and anchor-free BEV regression presents a distinct advancement over proposal-based trackers. The design effectively addresses sparsity challenges, and ablation studies rigorously validate key components (e.g., voxel size, template schemes).  
- **Strong Empirical Results**: The method achieves impressive performance gains (e.g., +10% Success on KITTI cars) and generalizes well across datasets. Results on sparse subsets highlight its robustness, advancing the field for real-world applications.  
- **Clarity & Reproducibility**: The paper is well-structured, with clear visualizations illustrating the architecture. The commitment to code release is commendable and essential for community adoption.  

### **Weaknesses**  
- **Performance Limitations**: The cyclist category underperforms, which the authors attribute to limited training data. However, sensitivity to data scarcity in other categories remains unaddressed. *Additionally, failure cases (e.g., sparse scenarios) warrant deeper analysis*.  
- **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: Parameters like voxel size and loss weights (λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) lack ablation studies, raising concerns about generalization to new datasets.  
- **Experimental Gaps**: *Inference latency versus baselines is unreported*, and real-time feasibility is unclear. Comparisons could include recent works (e.g., M²-Track).  
- **Presentation Issues**: The color scheme in Fig. 3 is ambiguous and should be clarified. Videos demonstrating tracking outputs (beyond static visualizations) would improve intuition.  

### **Limitations and Societal Impact**  
- **Technical Limitations**: The computational cost of shape generation during training is noted, but *inference efficiency and scalability to unseen object categories are unquantified*. The reliance on hyperparameters may hinder deployment in new environments.  
- **Broader Impact**: While advancing autonomous driving safety, the paper omits critical discussions on:  
  - **Ethical Risks**: Potential misuse in surveillance or safety-critical failures from tracking errors.  
  - **Sensor Fusion**: The authors should discuss why RGB data isn’t integrated despite safety imperatives and how V2B could extend to multimodal inputs.  
  - **Environmental Costs**: Energy consumption of LiDAR systems merits consideration.  

### **Recommendation**  
The paper presents a technically sound and novel approach with strong empirical results. However, unresolved issues—limited cyclist performance, unanalyzed hyperparameters, omitted inference metrics, and insufficient societal impact discussion—prevent a higher rating. **I recommend acceptance as is (Rating: 6—Marginally above acceptance threshold)**, contingent on the authors:  
1. Clarifying Fig. 3’s color scheme and providing tracking videos.  
2. Addressing hyperparameter sensitivity and generalization concerns.  
3. Adding ethical considerations and computational trade-offs (e.g., inference latency) in the camera-ready version.  
4. Honoring their code-release commitment to ensure reproducibility.  

---  
**Rating**: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review: V2B - A Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Single Object Tracking in Sparse Point Clouds  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single-object tracking in LiDAR point clouds. The method adopts a tracking-by-detection framework featuring: (1) a shape-aware feature learning network that enhances target discrimination through template feature embedding and dense shape generation (trained with a regularization shape completion loss), and (2) a voxel-to-BEV localization network that regresses target centers directly from dense bird's-eye-view features without proposals. Experiments on KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in sparse scenarios, with significant improvements over methods like P2B and SC3D.  

**Main Review:**  
The paper presents a technically sound approach to a relevant problem, advancing sparse-scene tracking performance by notable margins (e.g., +10% Success on KITTI cars). The anchor-free BEV regression and integration of shape completion are promising innovations. However, the following aspects require substantial improvement to meet publication standards:  

1.  **Novelty Clarification and Comparative Analysis:**  
    The method builds heavily on P2B (Siamese framework, template embedding) and SC3D (shape completion regularization loss). While the *combination* of shape-aware learning and BEV-based center regression is novel, the manuscript inadequately distinguishes core contributions from prior work. Specifically:  
    - The dense BEV localization replaces P2B's proposal-based approach, but lacks qualitative visualizations (e.g., Figure 1 could show improved sparse-object localization).  
    - Modifications to SC3D's shape completion loss (Lines 187-190) require ablation to justify design choices and establish novelty. Include an ablation study on loss weights (λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) and the impact of discarding shape encoding during inference.  
    - Recent anchor-free 3D detection paradigms (e.g., M²-Track) and speed comparisons (P2B’s 40FPS vs. reported 17FPS on superior hardware) need discussion. Clarify inherited elements versus novel adaptations.  

2.  **Methodology Presentation:**  
    While the technical approach is rigorous, the method section is currently hard to follow due to dense technical details without sufficient motivation. Restructure to:  
    - Better contextualize architectural choices (e.g., *why* voxel-to-BEV pooling was chosen over alternatives).  
    -

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The method integrates global and local template matching, a shape-completion network to enhance target discrimination through dense point cloud generation, and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that regresses 3D centers directly from compressed bird's-eye-view features without proposals. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in sparse scenarios, with significant gains (e.g., +8.9% mean Success on KITTI).  

**Main Review:**  
The results are promising, showing strong empirical validation across datasets and sparse/dense splits. The technical approach is rigorous, with ablation studies confirming the value of components like shape-aware feature learning and the voxel-to-BEV design. The method’s efficiency in eliminating proposal generation and its anchor-free regression advance practical applications in autonomous driving.  

However, **the core novelty is limited**. The proposed framework ensembles established concepts: template matching (e.g., SiamRPN [1]), shape-completion, and BEV localization (e.g., LaserNet [2]). While the *combination* of these elements is well-executed, the individual innovations are incremental. For instance, the anchor-free BEV regression builds on recent detection paradigms, and the shape-completion module resembles prior work in point cloud densification. The ablation studies appropriately validate design choices like voxel size and template schemes, but they lack analysis of critical hyperparameters (e.g., loss weights λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) and failure cases in extreme sparsity.  

Further, computational trade-offs are insufficiently explored. The shape-completion and voxelization steps incur unquantified inference-time latency, which is crucial for real-world deployment. While the code release ensures reproducibility, comparisons of inference speed versus baselines (e.g., P2B, BAT) are absent.  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The authors acknowledge sensitivity to sparse point clouds but overlook broader limitations:  
- Generalization to unseen object categories is untested.  
- Computational costs (energy, latency) could hinder real-time applications.  
Societal impacts are unaddressed, such as safety risks from tracking failures in autonomous vehicles or potential misuse in surveillance. Ethical guidelines for deployment should be discussed.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The work is technically sound and delivers strong performance, particularly in sparse scenarios. However, the modest novelty and unexamined computational/ethical constraints limit its broader impact. With minor revisions (e.g., hyperparameter analysis, societal implications), this could be a more compelling contribution.  

---  
**References:**  
[1] Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." *CVPR* 2018.  
[2] Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." *CVPR* 2019.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary  
This paper proposes V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single-object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The method incorporates shape completion to enhance feature learning and a voxel-to-BEV localization network for anchor-free center regression. Evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate performance improvements over baselines, particularly in sparse scenarios.  

### Strengths  
1. **Effective Feature Engineering**: The design of local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction is intuitive and significantly improves performance, as validated by ablation studies (Table 4).  
2. **Robust Qualitative Analysis**: Visualizations effectively demonstrate the method’s utility in sparse point cloud scenarios.  
3. **Novel Integration**: The combination of shape-aware feature learning (via dense shape generation) and BEV-based center regression is a distinct contribution, eliminating proposal generation and advancing sparse-scene tracking.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Unclear Contribution Significance**:  
   a) *Shape Completion*: While ablation studies confirm the importance of shape completion in the search area (Table 4), the paper lacks a direct comparison with [13]’s template-based approach. The claim that search-area completion is superior remains unsupported, as differences in codebases and implementation details (e.g., hyperparameters) could explain performance gaps in Table 1. A controlled experiment comparing both paradigms within the same framework is needed.  
   b) *Voxel-to-BEV Design*: The use of max-pooling along the *z*-axis to generate BEV features is inadequately justified. Intuitively, discarding *z*-axis information via pooling should hinder 3D center regression, contradicting the claim in Line 68. Ablation studies should quantify the impact on *z*-axis localization accuracy, not just final performance.  
2. **Outdated Network Design**: The reliance on PointNet++ (NeurIPS 2017) is not justified, especially given newer point cloud methods (e.g., M²-Track, ECCV 2022). The paper should address why more recent architectures were not adopted.  
3. **Insufficient Evaluation Rigor**:  
   - Testing on only two KITTI sequences is inadequate, as the dataset is small and category-specific results (e.g., Van, Cyclist) suffer from high variance. While nuScenes results are included (as noted in alternative reviews), the paper’s primary analysis still centers on KITTI. Expanding evaluation to larger datasets like Waymo or more nuScenes categories would strengthen robustness.  
   - Recent baselines (post-2021) are missing from comparisons, limiting the claim of state-of-the-art performance.  

### Limitations and Societal Impact  
**Limitations**: The computational cost of shape completion during training and sensitivity to voxel-size hyperparameters are acknowledged but unquantified. Inference latency versus baselines is unreported, raising concerns about real-world feasibility. Generalization to unseen object categories is also untested.  
**Societal Impact**: While negative societal impacts (e.g., safety risks from tracking failures in autonomous systems) are not discussed, this omission is acceptable given the paper’s scope. A brief discussion of ethical guidelines for deployment would be beneficial.  

### Recommendation  
The paper presents a technically sound approach with promising results on sparse data. However, critical gaps remain in justifying design choices (e.g., BEV pooling, PointNet++), validating contributions against relevant baselines ([13]), and ensuring evaluation robustness. These issues limit the work’s significance despite its novel integration of shape completion and BEV regression.  
**Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)**.  
*Conditional on addressing the weaknesses, particularly by adding: (1) direct shape completion comparisons with [13], (2) ablation studies on BEV’s impact on z-axis localization, and (3) expanded dataset evaluations.*  

---  
**Interleaving Notes**:  
- **Strengths**: Retained main review’s focus on feature engineering/qualitative results, while integrating alternatives’ emphasis on novelty (shape completion + BEV) and multi-dataset validation.  
- **Weaknesses**: Main review’s critiques (unclear contributions, BEV justification, outdated design, limited evaluation) form the core. Alternatives’ observations (missing recent baselines, unquantified hyperparameters) are woven into Points 1b, 2, and 3.  
- **Limitations**: Combined main review’s sparsity concerns with alternatives’ computational cost/real-time feasibility points.  
- **Decision**: Unchanged from main review (6), with revisions framed as essential for significance.  
- **Tone**: Consistently critical but constructive, aligning with the main review’s stance.

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes the Siamese Voxel-to-Bird’s Eye View (V2B) tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The framework employs a Siamese network to extract point features from template and search areas, adaptively learns correlations to embed template features into potential objects, and incorporates shape-aware feature learning via dense point cloud generation. It decomposes 3D center regression into 2D BEV and z-axis components. While the authors claim state-of-the-art results on KITTI and nuScenes datasets, significant concerns remain regarding the method’s characterization as a tracking framework, breadth of evaluation, and contextualization within the field.  

### Critique  
**1. Clarity and Scope:**  
The paper is generally well-structured, with clear technical descriptions of the shape-aware feature learning and voxel-to-BEV localization components. However, the core premise as a "tracking framework" is **fundamentally unconvincing**. The work lacks essential tracking mechanics (e.g., track initiation/termination, occlusion handling, identity management) and focuses solely on single-frame object localization relative to an initial template. While the shape-aware learning (via dense point generation) and anchor-free BEV regression are technically sound, they align more closely with *detection* than tracking. The absence of multi-object tracking (MOT) capabilities or evaluation against MOT benchmarks (e.g., MOTA, HOTA) further weakens this claim. Notably, the gate mechanism (Eq. 5) and inference-time discarding of shape encoding lack detailed justification, exacerbating confusion about the method’s tracking applicability.  

**2. Related Work:**  
The literature review is **incomplete and narrowly focused**. While recent Siamese trackers (e.g., P2B, SC3D) are cited, critical gaps exist:  
- Omission of foundational 3D MOT works (e.g., AB3DMOT, EagerMOT) and benchmarks (Waymo, JRDB).  
- No discussion of anchor-free 3D detection paradigms that share similarities with the BEV approach.  
- Missing comparisons to contemporary trackers like M²-Track (ECCV 2022).  
This selectivity undermines the claimed novelty and prevents a rigorous contextualization of contributions.  

**3. Originality and Contribution:**  
The integration of shape completion and BEV-based center regression addresses sparsity challenges effectively, and the anchor-free design offers efficiency advantages. However, these are **incremental adaptations** of existing techniques rather than transformative contributions. The method combines established concepts (Siamese networks, BEV representations, shape completion) without new theoretical insights. While application papers are valuable, they necessitate exhaustive empirical validation across diverse scenarios—which this work lacks.  

**4. Experiments and Evaluation:**  
Experimental validation is **insufficient for a tracking paper**:  
- **Datasets:** Only KITTI and nuScenes are tested, ignoring key benchmarks (JRDB, Waymo). Notably, nuScenes results are reported but lack category-wise breakdowns (Table 1) and critical MOT metrics.  
- **Metrics:** The use of Success/Precision ignores standard 3D MOT metrics (MOTA, HOTA), preventing direct comparison to state-of-the-art trackers.  
- **Scope:** The method is not evaluated for non-rigid objects (e.g., pedestrians on JRDB) or multi-object scenarios.  
- **Technical Gaps:** Computational costs (e.g., shape generation latency) and hyperparameter sensitivity (voxel size, loss weights λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) are unquantified. Real-time feasibility remains unaddressed despite relevance for autonomous driving.  
While ablation studies validate design choices (e.g., voxel size, template schemes), the absence of failure-case analysis and inference-speed comparisons to baselines limits reproducibility.  

**5. Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The authors acknowledge sparsity limitations but **overlook critical issues**:  
- **Ethical Risks:** Potential misuse in surveillance or human monitoring is unaddressed.  
- **Safety:** Tracking failures in safety-critical systems (e.g., autonomous vehicles) could have severe societal consequences.  
- **Resource Costs:** Energy consumption of LiDAR systems and computational trade-offs are ignored.  

### Recommendation  
While the technical approach (shape-aware learning, BEV regression) demonstrates merit in sparse point cloud processing, the paper **fails to establish itself as a cohesive tracking framework**. The conflation of detection and tracking, limited evaluation against MOT standards, and narrow literature review significantly diminish its impact. For NeurIPS, where rigorous novelty and comprehensive benchmarking are expected, these shortcomings are prohibitive. The work may be better suited for a venue focused on perception or detection.  

**Rating: 4 (Reject)**  
*Ok but not good enough—rejection*  

---

### Key Integrations from Alternative Reviews  
- **Strengths:** Acknowledged shape-aware learning novelty, BEV efficiency, and strong KITTI/nuScenes results (§1, §3).  
- **Weaknesses:**  
  - Added critiques on unanalyzed hyperparameters (λ₁,λ₂,λ₃) and computational costs (§4).  
  - Noted missing MOT metrics and real-time analysis (§4).  
  - Highlighted gaps in ethical/societal discussion (§5).  
- **Structure:** Interleaved positive observations (e.g., ablation studies) with the main review’s core criticisms to maintain coherence while preserving the rejection rationale.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. (quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos:
